key_insight: Under a single nanoscratch, 316H stainless steel follows a pressure- and shear-dependent martensitic sequence, with hydrostatic compression favoring γ→ε and lateral sliding enabling ε→α′.
engineering_relevance: Understanding how contact stress state selects martensitic pathways can guide the design of wear- and galling-resistant alloys and surface treatments for tribological components.
method: In-situ synchrotron X-ray nanodiffractometry during nanoscratch testing
Finite element modelling to map the evolving stress/strain field beneath the contact
Spatially resolved phase identification of γ, ε, and α′ martensite in the tribolayer
evidence_signal: The abstract reports spatial maps from a single sliding asperity that link measured diffraction signatures to FE-interpreted stress states and phase distributions.
watch_item: The conclusions are derived from localized nanoscratch conditions, so translation to real multi-asperity, larger-scale service contacts may require additional validation.
paper_title: Spatially resolved in-situ characterisation of competing martensitic transformation pathways during nanoscratch in 316H Stainless Steel
